skill.name: academic-poster-generator
skill.description: Complete workflow for generating academic research posters from PDF literature; use when you need to extract paper content from PDFs and produce a LaTeX-based poster (beamerposter/tikzposter/baposter) with mandatory figure generation and a final rendered HTML deliverable.
skill.summary: Turn research paper PDFs into academic conference posters with LaTeX templates, auto-generated figures, and HTML output.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: This skill is a legitimate academic poster generation tool from the AIPOCH organization. Static analysis detected 812 potential issues, but the vast majority are false positives caused by pattern matching on LaTeX template files, markdown reference documentation, and legitimate Python operations. The skill uses subprocess.run for LaTeX compilation, PDF conversion, and image processing, which is expected behavior. One concern is the use of shell=True in review_poster.py. All commands use hardcoded or argparse-controlled arguments. No evidence of malicious intent, data exfiltration, or credential theft was found.

high_findings.title: External Command Execution via subprocess.run
high_findings.description: Multiple Python scripts use subprocess.run to execute external commands (pdflatex, pandoc, pdf2htmlEX, python scripts). Arguments come from argparse and are hardcoded, not from untrusted user input. This is expected behavior for a LaTeX poster generation tool. Review_poster.py uses shell=True which increases risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run is used for legitimate LaTeX/pdf processing tools, but review_poster.py uses shell=True. Arguments are controlled and not from direct user input.

medium_findings.title: File System Write Operations
medium_findings.description: Python scripts write output files (JSON configs, generated figures, text content) to the filesystem. This is expected behavior for an academic poster generator that produces deliverable artifacts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: File write operations are core to the skill's functionality. Operations write to output directories with controlled paths. No evidence of unauthorized file access or overwrite.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL in LaTeX Template
low_findings.description: LaTeX template contains a QR code placeholder URL pointing to a GitHub repository. This is a template placeholder and does not execute network requests during compilation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: QR code URL is a template placeholder intended for user replacement. Not an actual network request.

low_findings.title: Reference Documentation Contains Shell Commands in Code Blocks
low_findings.description: Multiple reference markdown files contain shell command examples in fenced code blocks. These are documentation examples, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All shell commands are inside markdown code blocks as documentation examples showing users how to use the tool. Not executable.

low_findings.title: Hex-Encoded Characters in Markdown Code Example
low_findings.description: Troubleshooting guide contains a Python regex pattern with hex character ranges for removing control characters from extracted text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hex characters are part of a Python regex pattern in a documentation code example for text cleaning. Not obfuscation.

low_findings.title: Bracket Chains in Python Code
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged bracket-chain patterns as potential JSFuck obfuscation. These are standard Python dictionary access patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 1 of both files is the shebang line. Bracket chains are Python dict/list indexing, not JavaScript obfuscation.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The review_poster.py script uses shell=True in subprocess.run, which increases command injection risk if user input reaches the command string. Current usage uses hardcoded commands, but shell=True should be avoided.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: shell=True is set but commands appear to be hardcoded. Pattern should be changed to use shell=False for defense in depth.

content.user_title: Generate academic conference posters from research PDFs
content.value_statement: Creating a conference poster from a research paper requires careful extraction of key findings and professional layout design. This skill automates the entire workflow: it extracts content from PDF papers, generates scientific figures, and produces a formatted LaTeX poster with HTML output ready for presentation.
actual_capabilities: Extract title, authors, abstract, and section structure from research paper PDFs
Generate 2-3 scientific figures (schematics, flowcharts, mechanism diagrams, comparison charts)
Create LaTeX posters using beamerposter, tikzposter, or baposter templates
Convert LaTeX posters to HTML using pandoc or pdf2htmlEX for browser viewing
Run automated quality checks on final output including image validation and layout integrity
Support end-to-end pipeline from PDF to rendered HTML poster in a single command
limitations: Requires system dependencies: Python 3.8+, LaTeX distribution, pandoc, and poppler utilities
OCR quality depends on pytesseract installation and input PDF scan quality
Figure generation produces matplotlib/PIL-based diagrams, not AI-generated custom artwork
HTML output requires manual CSS review for WCAG AA compliance (automated check provided)

use_cases.title: Conference poster from published research
use_cases.description: A researcher prepares a poster for an upcoming conference by providing the published paper PDF. The skill extracts key sections, generates supporting figures, and produces a formatted poster with professional layout.

use_cases.title: Lab meeting poster from internal report
use_cases.description: A graduate student needs to present preliminary findings at a lab meeting. The skill converts an internal research document into a structured poster with methodology diagrams and results charts.

use_cases.title: Literature review poster for journal club
use_cases.description: A journal club member summarizes a recent publication for group discussion. The skill creates a poster highlighting the study design, key results, and conclusions in an accessible visual format.

prompt_templates.title: Basic poster from PDF
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the academic poster generator pipeline on this PDF research paper and produce a beamerposter-style conference poster with auto-generated figures.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick poster generation from a single PDF paper

prompt_templates.title: Custom template poster
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract content from this PDF and create a tikzposter poster. Use the poster layout design guide from references/poster_layout_design.md for layout decisions. Generate 3 figures including a mechanism diagram and a comparison chart.
prompt_templates.scenario: Poster with specific template and figure requirements

prompt_templates.title: HTML-only poster output
prompt_templates.prompt: Process this PDF through the full pipeline but skip PDF compilation. Output only the HTML version. Run quality checks with check_poster_quality.py on the final HTML output.

prompt_templates.title: Custom figures from config file
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the poster pipeline with this figures_config.json to generate 3 custom figures: a schematic of the signaling pathway, a flowchart of the experimental design, and a comparison chart of control vs treatment results. Use baposter template.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced poster with user-defined figure specifications

output_examples.input: A PDF research paper about cancer cell metabolism with 5 sections and 3 data tables
output: poster.rendered.html - Browser-ready conference poster with 3-column layout
figures/mechanism.png - Pathway diagram showing metabolic reprogramming steps
figures/process_flowchart.png - Experimental workflow from sample collection to analysis
figures/comparison_chart.png - Bar chart comparing control vs treatment group metrics

output_examples.input: A clinical trial paper PDF with abstract, methods, results, and discussion sections
output: Structured LaTeX poster with Introduction, Methods, Results, Conclusions blocks
Auto-extracted title, authors, and institution from PDF metadata
Bullet-point formatted content adapted to poster word budget (600-800 words)
Quality check report confirming image presence, layout integrity, and contrast compliance

best_practices: Run the full pipeline with run_poster_pipeline.py to ensure all stages execute in the correct order with consistent output paths
Always run check_poster_quality.py on the final HTML output to validate images, layout, and WCAG AA contrast before delivery
Review and edit the auto-generated CSS in the final HTML to ensure proper mobile responsiveness and column layout
anti_patterns: Returning intermediate artifacts (metadata.json, poster.tex, poster.html) to the user instead of only the final poster.rendered.html and figures/ directory
Skipping figure generation or quality checks to save time - the pipeline requires at least 2-3 figures and quality validation
Editing LaTeX templates directly instead of using the structured content workflow for consistent formatting

faq.question: What system dependencies are needed to run this skill?
faq.answer: You need Python 3.8+ with pypdf, pdfplumber, pdf2image, pytesseract, Pillow, and matplotlib. System tools: tesseract-ocr and poppler utilities. LaTeX and pandoc are optional depending on output format.

faq.question: Can this skill generate posters from scanned PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes, if pytesseract and tesseract-ocr are installed. The pipeline uses OCR to extract text from scanned PDF pages. OCR quality depends on the scan resolution and clarity.

faq.question: What poster templates are available?
faq.answer: Three LaTeX templates are included: beamerposter (traditional academic), tikzposter (modern colorful), and baposter (multi-column layouts). Templates are in assets/templates/.

faq.question: Does the skill compile LaTeX to PDF?
faq.answer: PDF compilation is optional. The default workflow creates HTML output using pandoc. Use --html-mode render for PDF-to-HTML conversion if pdf2htmlEX is installed.

faq.question: How many figures are generated for each poster?
faq.answer: The pipeline generates at least 2-3 figures by default: a mechanism diagram, a process flowchart, and a comparison chart. You can customize figures using a figures_config.json file.

faq.question: What quality checks are performed on the output?
faq.answer: The check_poster_quality.py script validates image existence, layout integrity, font readability, WCAG AA contrast compliance (4.5:1), and confirms no placeholder text remains.
